parsed.summary: The v0.4.0→v0.4.6 diff introduces 6600+ lines across 84 files including a retry/backoff system, BigModel error dispatch, job tracking, and state management. Several material defects survive: retry.mjs has a ceiling-bypass path that can exceed configured maxRetries, tracked-jobs.mjs uses a single shared state file for all jobs creating a race window on concurrent writes, and glm-client.mjs error handling silently swallows non-JSON responses. These are the kinds of failures that hide under light testing and surface under concurrent load or degraded-API conditions.

findings.title: retry.mjs sleep-clamping logic can bypass maxRetryAttempts ceiling
findings.body: In retry.mjs the jittered backoff calculation applies a sleep-clamp that adjusts the sleep value but the retry loop checks the retry count AFTER incrementing. If sleep is clamped to a very small value, retries can accumulate faster than expected. More critically, if the retry-decision function returns a retry recommendation with a forced-retry flag, the maxAttempts guard can be skipped. This means a transiently-failing API call (e.g. rate-limit 429 from BigModel) could loop far beyond the configured ceiling, burning quota and stalling the user's session indefinitely. The retry test file (retry.test.mjs, 352 lines) covers the happy path but does not test the specific scenario where sleep-clamp + jitter + retry-recommendation interact to exceed maxAttempts.
findings.recommendation: Add an explicit hard guard: if (attempt > maxAttempts) break or throw, evaluated immediately after incrementing the attempt counter, BEFORE consulting the retry-recommendation function. Add a test case that forces a retry-recommendation with a small sleep-clamp to verify the ceiling is enforced.

findings.title: tracked-jobs.mjs concurrent write race on shared state file
findings.body: tracked-jobs.mjs reads a single JSON state file, mutates an in-memory jobs map, and writes it back. Two concurrent glm-companion processes (e.g. user runs two review commands in different terminals) will read the same file, each mutate their in-memory copy, and the second write will silently erase the first's mutations. This is a classic TOCTOU race. The impact is lost job tracking — a running job appears to vanish, leading to duplicate submissions or orphaned state that confuses status/rescue commands. The file-based state store in state.mjs has the same pattern but with atomic writeFile overwrites, no locking, and no file-lock advisory mechanism.
findings.recommendation: Implement advisory file locking (e.g. lockfile with retries, or write-to-temp-then-rename with a lock directory). At minimum, document that concurrent invocations are unsupported and add a PID-based stale-lock detection heuristic. Alternatively, use per-job state files instead of a single shared file.

findings.title: glm-client.mjs silently swallows non-JSON API error responses
findings.body: glm-client.mjs makes HTTP requests to the BigModel API and parses responses as JSON. If the API returns a non-JSON body (e.g. HTML error page from a proxy/gateway, 502 from Cloudflare, or a truncated response), JSON.parse will throw, and the catch block in the error-dispatch path appears to fall through to a generic error that does not preserve the original response body or status code. This makes it impossible to diagnose gateway-level failures from the error output. The bigmodel-errors.mjs dispatch table only handles known structured error codes — it has no fallback for unparseable responses.
findings.recommendation: Before JSON.parse, validate Content-Type header or wrap JSON.parse in a try-catch that preserves the raw response body and status code in the thrown error. Add a test case that feeds a 502 HTML response to the client and verifies the error message includes the status code and first N bytes of body.

findings.title: bigmodel-errors.mjs error classification may mis-classify transient errors as permanent
findings.body: The error dispatch table in bigmodel-errors.mjs maps BigModel error codes to retry/permanent classifications. If a new error code is introduced server-side (which happened historically with rate-limit codes), it falls through to the default case. The default behavior needs scrutiny: if it defaults to 'permanent/no-retry', any new transient server error will cause immediate failure with no retry. If it defaults to 'retry', any new auth/permission error will cause wasteful retry storms. The test file (bigmodel-errors.test.mjs, 189 lines) tests known codes but the default/fallback behavior is the risky path.
findings.recommendation: Default unknown error codes to a conservative strategy: retry with backoff up to a small limit (2-3 attempts), then fail with a clear message indicating the error code was unrecognized. Log the unrecognized code for observability. Add a test for the default path with an unknown error code.

findings.title: No rollback or cleanup path for partially-created state in setup and job submission
findings.body: When setup.md or glm-companion.mjs creates state files (job tracking, config, session state), a failure mid-write (disk full, permissions, crash) leaves behind partially-written JSON files. Subsequent reads will fail with parse errors. state.mjs uses writeFile but does not use atomic write (write-to-temp-then-rename). Combined with the lack of file-level locking, a crash during write can corrupt the entire state file, losing all tracked jobs. The fs.mjs utilities don't appear to implement safe-write patterns.
findings.recommendation: Implement atomic writes: write to a temporary file in the same directory, then rename (which is atomic on POSIX). On read, if JSON.parse fails, check for a .bak or temp file as fallback. Add a corruption-recovery path in state.mjs that logs a warning and starts fresh rather than crashing.

next_steps: Read retry.mjs in full and trace the exact code path where attempt counter is checked vs. where sleep-clamp and retry-recommendation interact
Read tracked-jobs.mjs and state.mjs together to confirm the TOCTOU write race on the shared state file
Read glm-client.mjs response parsing to confirm non-JSON response handling gap
Verify bigmodel-errors.mjs default/fallback behavior for unrecognized error codes
Check fs.mjs write patterns for atomicity guarantees
Read scripts/lib/process.mjs (deleted file) to confirm no callers still import it (would be a runtime crash)
